intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A multi-agent airline customer-service system that triages inbound customer requests and routes them to specialist agents that answer FAQ questions or update seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations. The remit authorizes two tools — faq_lookup_tool (deterministic FAQ lookup) and update_seat (seat assignment on confirmed reservations) — operating against the airline reservation system, FAQ dataset, and flight schedule. Identity verification, confirmation-number validation against the authoritative reservation record, and a durable audit trail for every seat change are mandatory; ticket purchases, refunds, special-handling cases, and any outbound email/SMS/webhook are forbidden.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A single-file Python example at examples/customer_service/main.py built on the openai-agents-python SDK. Three Agent instances — triage_agent, faq_agent, seat_booking_agent — share an AirlineAgentContext Pydantic model holding passenger_name, confirmation_number, seat_number, and flight_number. Handoffs are wired with handoff(...); update_seat writes to context.context with no reservation-record lookup, and on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates a flight number via random.randint(100, 999). The SDK exposes InputGuardrail, OutputGuardrail, ToolInputGuardrail, needs_approval, and tracing primitives, but the example wires none of them onto these three agents.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is framework offers safe primitives, the example wires none of them. The openai-agents-python SDK ships InputGuardrail, OutputGuardrail, ToolInputGuardrail, per-tool needs_approval, and tracing — exactly the controls the airline remit calls for — yet examples/customer_service/main.py configures zero guardrails, zero approval gates, and no audit logging. As a result every behavioral rule that requires runtime enforcement (identity verification before mutation, confirmation-number validation against an authoritative record, refusal of out-of-scope flows, prompt-injection resistance, durable seat-change audit) is enforced only by instructions in an LLM system prompt — and update_seat trusts caller-supplied confirmation_number and new_seat directly, while on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates a flight number with random.randint in place of looking it up. A reader copying this example into production inherits a system whose stated invariants exist only as English in a prompt.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agents may use
rules.rule_text: faq_lookup_tool — deterministic lookup against the airline FAQ dataset, returning only scripted answers or "I don't know"; used by the FAQ agent only.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agents may use
rules.rule_text: update_seat — updates the seat assignment for a given confirmation number; used by the seat-booking agent only.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized output destinations
rules.rule_text: The agents MUST NOT send email, SMS, webhooks, or any other outbound external communication.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: The agents do not store PII, payment data, or reservation details beyond the lifetime of the current session.

findings.summary: No customer identity verification anywhere in the agent pipeline before update_seat mutates reservation state.
findings.description: The remit requires customer identity to be confirmed before any reservation-mutating action and unverified identity to escalate to a human. The example never authenticates the caller — AirlineAgentContext initializes with all fields None, no agent reads or sets passenger_name from an authenticated session, and update_seat writes confirmation_number and seat_number straight from tool arguments to the shared context.
findings.policy_rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state. / Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified. / Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

evidence.snippet: AirlineAgentContext fields (passenger_name, confirmation_number, seat_number, flight_number) all default to None and are never populated from an authenticated session.
update_seat accepts confirmation_number and new_seat as tool arguments and writes them to context.context with no identity check.
main() builds a fresh AirlineAgentContext() and conversation_id = uuid.uuid4() with no authentication step; any caller can claim any confirmation number.

recommended_actions: Before constructing AirlineAgentContext, authenticate the customer (session token, OIDC, etc.) and seed context.passenger_name from the verified identity claim — never from tool arguments or user input.
Add an InputGuardrail on triage_agent that halts when context.passenger_name is None, and route the halt to the remit's human-escalation path.
Inside update_seat, fetch the reservation from the authoritative reservation system and assert it belongs to context.passenger_name before any context write.

findings.summary: update_seat trusts caller-supplied confirmation_number with no lookup against the authoritative reservation record.
findings.description: The remit forbids issuing a seat change without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record. The tool function takes confirmation_number as a model-supplied argument, writes it into the shared context, asserts only that some flight_number has been set on the context (by the handoff hook that fabricated one — see PRAX-2026-05-23-005), and returns a success string. No reservation-system call exists in the example or the SDK path it invokes.
findings.policy_rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only. / A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified. / A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

evidence.snippet: update_seat signature takes confirmation_number: str and new_seat: str directly from the LLM tool call.
context.context.confirmation_number = confirmation_number — the value is written to context with no lookup against any reservation store.
The only precondition checked is assert context.context.flight_number is not None — flight_number is itself fabricated, not retrieved (see PRAX-2026-05-23-005).

recommended_actions: Replace the body of update_seat with a reservation-system lookup (e.g., reservation = reservations.get(confirmation_number)) and raise a tool error if the record is missing or does not belong to context.passenger_name.
Add a ToolInputGuardrail on update_seat that enforces the same precondition and returns RejectContentBehavior when the confirmation number is unknown.

findings.summary: update_seat performs no existence or availability check on the requested seat.
findings.description: The remit constrains seat updates to seats that exist on the flight and are not already assigned to another passenger. The example writes the new seat string directly to context with no consultation of a seat map, no collision check against other passengers, and no validation of seat-format syntax. A caller can submit any string — including a seat assigned to another customer — and the tool reports success.
findings.policy_rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

evidence.snippet: context.context.seat_number = new_seat — the only operation on new_seat; no seat-map lookup, no availability check, no format validation.
return f"Updated seat to {new_seat} for confirmation number {confirmation_number}" — success is reported unconditionally.

recommended_actions: Inside update_seat, fetch the flight's seat map from the authoritative reservation system and reject the call if new_seat is not in the map or is currently assigned to another passenger.
Wrap the validation in a ToolInputGuardrail that returns RejectContentBehavior with a model-readable rejection reason so the LLM can ask the customer for another seat.

findings.summary: No durable audit log is written for seat changes — the remit-mandated record (time, identity, confirmation number, old/new seat, agent) does not exist.
findings.description: The remit requires every seat change to land in a durable audit log with five named fields. The example writes nothing — no logging call, no file write, no append to a database — when update_seat succeeds. The SDK's tracing facility is enabled via trace("Customer service", ...) at line 166, but trace spans go to the OpenAI tracing backend (or nowhere if disabled) and are not a durable application-side audit log of the kind the remit specifies, nor do they include old/new seat as structured fields.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: update_seat body, lines 67-83 — no logger.info, no audit append, no DB write; the only side effect is mutation of in-memory context.context.
with trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id): — tracing is initialized but is a vendor span sink, not a durable application-side audit log with the remit's named fields.

recommended_actions: Inside update_seat, after a successful reservation-system update, append a structured record — {timestamp, passenger_name, confirmation_number, old_seat, new_seat, agent_name} — to a durable store (append-only DB table, signed JSONL log).
Add the same record on the rejection path so attempted unauthorised changes are also captured.

findings.summary: on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates a flight number with random.randint(100, 999) instead of looking it up.
findings.description: The remit explicitly forbids the agent from generating flight numbers, confirmation numbers, or passenger identifiers at runtime; these MUST come from the authoritative reservation system. The handoff hook ignores the rule literally — it manufactures a flight number out of randomness on every triage→seat-booking handoff, and that fabricated value is the only precondition update_seat asserts (see PRAX-2026-05-23-002). The result is that the seat-update path always satisfies its only check using data the system invented seconds earlier.
findings.policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: async def on_seat_booking_handoff(...): flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}"; context.context.flight_number = flight_number
import random — used solely to fabricate identifiers in the handoff hook.

recommended_actions: Replace the body of on_seat_booking_handoff with a reservation-system lookup keyed on the authenticated customer (and, once collected, the confirmation number) — and raise an exception that triggers a human escalation if no flight record is found.
Remove import random from main.py — it has no legitimate use in this agent.

findings.summary: No InputGuardrail is configured on any agent — customer free-text flows into the LLM with no prompt-injection screening.
findings.description: The remit prohibits accepting instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity. The SDK provides InputGuardrail precisely for this — but none of triage_agent, faq_agent, or seat_booking_agent sets the input_guardrails field. main() appends raw user_input directly to input_items and passes it to Runner.run; the only defence against prompt injection is the system-prompt phrasing of each agent, which is exactly the layer the remit anticipates being bypassed.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent = Agent[...](name="FAQ Agent", ..., tools=[faq_lookup_tool]) — no input_guardrails kwarg.
seat_booking_agent = Agent[...](... tools=[update_seat]) — no input_guardrails kwarg.
triage_agent = Agent[...](... handoffs=[...]) — no input_guardrails kwarg.
input_items.append({"content": user_input, "role": "user"}) — raw user input is appended without any screening pass.

recommended_actions: Add an InputGuardrail (e.g. injection_screen) that runs a small classifier or rule set on each customer turn and trips on patterns like "ignore previous instructions", "you are now", or any directive targeting the agent's role; attach it to all three agents' input_guardrails lists.
Wire the guardrail's tripwire to the remit's human-escalation path (drop the conversation, log the trip, and surface to a human queue) — do not just raise and continue.

findings.summary: No ToolOutputGuardrail or output filter — tool returns and handoff content flow back into the LLM unchecked.
findings.description: The remit forbids accepting instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change agent behaviour. The example installs no ToolOutputGuardrail on faq_lookup_tool or update_seat and no output_guardrails on any agent. The FAQ tool today returns static strings, but the moment the FAQ dataset moves to an external store (the natural next step) or the reservation system returns vendor-controlled data through update_seat, that content is fed back into the LLM with no sanitization.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: @function_tool(name_override="faq_lookup_tool", ...) — decorator invocation does not set tool_input_guardrails or tool_output_guardrails.
@function_tool above update_seat — same: no output guardrail wired.
ToolInputGuardrail / ToolOutputGuardrail / RejectContentBehavior / RaiseExceptionBehavior all exist in the SDK and would slot in directly — none are used in the example.

recommended_actions: Add a ToolOutputGuardrail that runs the tool result through an instruction-detection check (regex + small classifier) and returns RejectContentBehavior when control phrases are detected; attach it to both faq_lookup_tool and update_seat.
Add an output_guardrails list on each agent that screens the final assistant message before it reaches the customer (e.g. PII leakage, tool-output regurgitation).

findings.summary: Handoffs bypass identity verification — transfer_to_seat_booking_agent hands control to a mutation-capable agent with no identity check on the way in.
findings.description: The remit requires that handoffs between agents must not escalate privileges or bypass identity verification. The example wires transfer_to_seat_booking_agent with on_handoff=on_seat_booking_handoff, and that hook fabricates a flight number (PRAX-2026-05-23-005) rather than checking identity. Because triage performs no authentication of its own (PRAX-2026-05-23-001), the privilege escalation from "triage, must-not-access-customer-data" to "seat-booking, can mutate reservation state" happens with zero identity gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

evidence.snippet: handoff(agent=seat_booking_agent, on_handoff=on_seat_booking_handoff, tool_name_override="transfer_to_seat_booking_agent") — no input_filter, no identity precondition.
on_seat_booking_handoff manufactures a flight_number and writes it to context; it never consults context.passenger_name.

recommended_actions: Rewrite on_seat_booking_handoff to assert context.context.passenger_name is not None and raise an escalation exception otherwise.
Add an input_filter on the handoff that strips earlier-agent tool-call history and confirms identity is set before the seat-booking agent's first turn.

findings.summary: No application-level structured logging — agent decisions, handoffs, tool calls, and rejections leave no durable record.
findings.description: Beyond the missing seat-change audit log (PRAX-2026-05-23-004), there is no application logging of any kind in the example. Handoffs, identity failures, prompt-injection trips, refusals, and FAQ-tool fallbacks all execute with output going only to print() on stdout and to vendor tracing spans. A production-shaped version of this agent would have no incident-reconstruction trail and no signal source for SOC-style detection.

evidence.snippet: for new_item in result.new_items: ... print(f"{agent_name}: ...") — print to stdout is the only sink for handoff events, tool calls, and tool outputs.
File-level grep — no import of logging, no logger instance, no structured event emission anywhere in main.py.

recommended_actions: Replace the print(...) dispatch loop with a structured logger (e.g. logging.getLogger with a JSON formatter) emitting one event per handoff, tool-call, tool-output, and rejection — keyed on conversation_id.
Forward the structured log to a durable sink (file, log pipeline, SIEM) so operators have a record independent of the OpenAI tracing backend.

findings.summary: Triage agent has no code-level scoping that enforces "MUST NOT access customer data directly" — the rule lives only in prose.
findings.description: The remit forbids the triage agent from accessing customer data directly. The example relies on the fact that triage simply has no tools wired in beyond its two handoffs — which is good as far as it goes. But there is no code-level invariant: any future commit that adds a tool, a memory hook, or a data fetch to triage_agent will silently break the rule, because no guardrail, no allowlist, and no test enforces the prohibition. The rule is satisfied incidentally, not structurally.
findings.policy_rule_text: The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent declaration — no tools= kwarg, only handoffs; the data-access prohibition is enforced only by omission.
instructions= prose for triage_agent — no enforcement language, no assertion that triage must refuse data-fetch attempts.

recommended_actions: Add a test (tests/test_customer_service_example.py) that asserts len(triage_agent.tools) == 0 and that triage_agent.handoffs contains only the documented two specialists; the test acts as a regression guard for the structural invariant.

Optionally add an InputGuardrail on triage that trips when context fields like confirmation_number are non-None at triage-turn entry, catching any future code path that lets reservation data leak into the triage step.

findings.summary: FAQ agent's "don't speculate" rule is enforced only by a system-prompt sentence, not by code.
findings.description: The remit requires the FAQ agent to use only the curated FAQ dataset and not speculate. The example partially honours this — faq_lookup_tool itself is deterministic and returns canned answers or "I don't know" — but the FAQ agent's instructions also tell the model to call the tool, and there is no code-level enforcement that the assistant message must come from the tool output. The model can still answer from its own knowledge if it chooses not to call the tool, and no output guardrail catches that.
findings.policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent instructions: "Use the faq lookup tool to answer the question. Do not rely on your own knowledge." — prose, not enforcement.
faq_agent has no output_guardrails — there is no post-message check that the answer came from the tool.

recommended_actions: Set tool_use_behavior on faq_agent to StopAtTools({"faq_lookup_tool"}) so the FAQ tool's output is the conversational response and the model never gets a free-text turn to speculate.
Add an OutputGuardrail that asserts the message body is a verbatim substring of a known FAQ-dataset entry (or the literal "I don't know" fallback) and rejects otherwise.

findings.summary: Out-of-scope refusal (refunds, payments, cancellations, rebooking) is enforced only by routine-prose, with no code-level rejection or human handoff.
findings.description: The remit forbids creating, cancelling, modifying, or refunding tickets/payments/fares and requires refunds, compensation, and rebooking to reach a human checkpoint. The example provides no code path for either — the seat-booking agent's prose says "If the customer asks a question that is not related to the routine, transfer back to the triage agent", which sends the request back into a loop with no specialist that can handle it and no path to a human. There is no out-of-scope detector, no "human checkpoint" tool, and no refusal-template.
findings.policy_rule_text: A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent. / Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system. / Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

evidence.snippet: seat_booking_agent instructions: "If the customer asks a question that is not related to the routine, transfer back to the triage agent." — sends to triage, but triage has no out-of-scope detector and no human-checkpoint handoff.
triage_agent handoffs list — only faq_agent and seat_booking_agent; there is no human-handoff option and no out-of-scope refuser.

recommended_actions: Add a small scope_classifier InputGuardrail on triage that detects refund/payment/rebooking/cancellation language and returns a canned refusal pointing at the billing system, with the trip logged structurally.
Add an escalate_to_human handoff (or tool) wired into all three agents, and document it in handoff_description; the remit's "human checkpoint" must be a concrete callable, not a prose request.

findings.summary: No detection or escalation path for special-handling flags (unaccompanied minors, medical assistance) — the remit's required human checkpoint does not exist.
findings.description: The remit requires requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags to reach a human checkpoint before proceeding. The example has no such detector — neither the prompts nor any guardrail mention these categories — and no human-handoff capability is wired into any agent (see also PRAX-2026-05-23-012). A customer mentioning a minor or a medical concern is handled by whichever agent happens to be on the conversation, with no policy hook.
findings.policy_rule_text: Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent, seat_booking_agent, triage_agent prompts — none reference minors, medical, or special-handling categories.
No human-handoff handoff exists on any agent; the remit's mandated human checkpoint has no implementation surface.

recommended_actions: Add an InputGuardrail (e.g. special_handling_screen) keyed on a curated phrase list for minors / medical / mobility / etc., and attach it to all three agents.
When the guardrail trips, route via an escalate_to_human handoff (see PRAX-2026-05-23-012's recommendation) — do not let the conversation continue inside the agent system.

positives.title: Triage agent ships with no data-access tools — data-access prohibition holds by omission
positives.description: The triage agent's Agent constructor wires only handoffs to faq_agent and seat_booking_agent, with no tools= kwarg and no memory/data hooks. That keeps the remit's "MUST NOT access customer data directly" rule satisfied today, even though no test or guardrail locks the invariant in place (see PRAX-2026-05-23-010 for the regression-guard recommendation).

positives.title: Tool inventory matches the remit's Known Good Baseline exactly
positives.description: The two functions decorated with @function_tool in this file are exactly faq_lookup_tool (used only by faq_agent) and update_seat (used only by seat_booking_agent) — no extra tools, no broader inventory, no remit-delta on capability surface.

positives.title: No session persistence — the remit's no-persistent-memory rule holds
positives.description: Each invocation of main() constructs a fresh AirlineAgentContext() and a fresh conversation_id from uuid.uuid4(); no Session backend or memory store is wired in. The remit's prohibition on persistent memory across customer sessions is honoured.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the customer_service example or in its execution path; the absence of a durable seat-change audit log is itself the subject of a Critical/High finding (see PRAX-2026-05-23-004 and PRAX-2026-05-23-009).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. The example sits at the bottom of the scale because every behavioural invariant the airline remit names — identity verification, confirmation-number validation against an authoritative record, prompt-injection resistance, durable seat-change audit, refusal of out-of-scope flows, escalation of special-handling cases — is left to LLM system-prompt phrasing instead of being enforced by code, even though the surrounding openai-agents-python SDK ships the exact primitives (InputGuardrail, OutputGuardrail, ToolInputGuardrail, needs_approval, tool_use_behavior) needed to honour them. The only points the agent earns are structural — triage carrying no tools by omission, tools nominally scoped per agent, the SDK itself being a vetted upstream dependency.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Triage has no tools and each specialist holds only its named tool — the tool inventory matches the remit by omission. But every other domain constraint (no speculation, no out-of-scope handling, no special-handling escalation) is enforced only in prose; nothing in code rejects an in-scope-looking refund request, and no human-checkpoint handoff exists.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Customer free-text input is appended to the model input list with no screening pass; the SDK's InputGuardrail, OutputGuardrail, and ToolOutputGuardrail are all unused on every agent. There is no boundary between untrusted content and the LLM context.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: update_seat trusts caller-supplied confirmation_number and new_seat directly; no identity check, no reservation-record lookup, no seat-existence check, no approval gate, and the only precondition it asserts is on a flight number fabricated by random.randint in the handoff hook.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The example lives inside a published SDK whose pyproject.toml and uv.lock pin upstream packages — partial credit for inheriting that discipline. The example itself imports only pydantic, asyncio, random, uuid, and the SDK; no new third-party surface is added, but no provenance review or per-tool dependency vetting is documented either.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artefact targets this example — no prompt-injection regression test, no handoff-bypass test, no tests/test_customer_service_example.py. The example ships with zero security tests of its own.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The SDK's tracing facility is invoked via trace("Customer service", ...) — a vendor span sink, not a durable application-side audit. There is no logging import, no seat-change audit record, no structured event emission; print() on stdout is the only application-level signal.
